episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Working Lands & Industry
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechRadar] Over 14 million login credentials leaked from six ISPs in major data breach — here’s what we know and [TechRadar] Google says Android's built-in earthquake warning system alerted 11.4 million people before the Venezuelan earthquake hit — here's how to find it on your phone, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Who captures economic value from working lands in the Cariboo, and what structural changes are actually enabling communities to retain more of that value? Topics include: Mining value capture and the Bunker Hill Idaho-to-Cariboo comparison, Indigenous title and legal leverage in resource negotiations post-Tŝilhqot'in decision, Carbon markets and income stacking on working lands, Capacity building and internal institutional ownership versus consultant dependency, Funding and grant treadmill problems in economic development projects.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Stampede Association

discussion.central_question: Who captures economic value from working lands in the Cariboo, and what structural changes are actually enabling communities to retain more of that value?
discussion.riley_position: New mechanisms exist to change value capture in resource development—particularly Indigenous title rights, carbon markets, and income stacking models—and the question is whether communities have the capacity and leverage to use them effectively.
riley_key_evidence: Osisko Mining in Malartic, Quebec developed explicit community benefit agreements and reclamation funds built into operating structures
Tŝilhqot'in Supreme Court decision fundamentally changed consultation and consent requirements for resource development on Aboriginal title land
Ranches can now stack multiple income streams: beef production, carbon sequestration, biodiversity offsets, and renewable energy development on the same land base
discussion.casey_position: The structural critiques about value bleeding from resource communities are not new, and real change requires communities to build permanent internal capacity rather than becoming dependent on external consultants and grant cycles.
casey_key_evidence: Bunker Hill mine cleanup costs were borne by taxpayers, not original operators, and the TSX-listed rebuild doesn't guarantee the town of Kellogg sees meaningful long-term economic benefit
The Malartic community benefit agreement emerged from resistance and conflict, not voluntary developer design, suggesting leverage matters more than goodwill
Most producer associations focus on regulatory advocacy and let new revenue development fall to whoever has a grant, leading to pilots that are abandoned when funding ends
discussion.resolution: They converged on the importance of permanent capacity-building over temporary projects, agreeing that internal ownership of verification infrastructure and planning functions—rather than external contracting—is what distinguishes temporary benefit from permanent change, and that grant-dependent project models should be replaced with evaluation criteria requiring documented internal capacity transfer.
topics_covered: Mining value capture and the Bunker Hill Idaho-to-Cariboo comparison
Indigenous title and legal leverage in resource negotiations post-Tŝilhqot'in decision
Carbon markets and income stacking on working lands
Capacity building and internal institutional ownership versus consultant dependency
Funding and grant treadmill problems in economic development projects
